plainMeaning: Those who cling for shelter to your two lofty feet, O Lord of sages, pay no mind even to a terrifying great elephant standing right beside them.
recitation: bhīmaṁ mahā gaïndaṁ, accāsannaṁ pi te na vi gaṇaṁti | je tumha calaṇa-jualaṁ, muṇivaï tuṅgaṁ samallīṇā ||

wordByWord.gloss: terrible, fearsome, dreadful (qualifying the elephant; accusative)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhīma 'terrible', from √bhī 'to fear'; Prakrit retains the form unchanged.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative singular agreeing with gaïndaṁ — the object that the devotee does not heed.
Intensifies the menace of the elephant: not merely an elephant but a great, towering one.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gaïnda < Skt. gajendra (gaja 'elephant' + indra 'lord/chief'); intervocalic -j- softens and the cluster simplifies.
wordByWord.notes: The peril of this verse-pair: the rutting elephant, here named as the very 'king' of elephants.

wordByWord.gloss: even though it is very close at hand
wordByWord.etymology: acca- (Skt. ati 'exceedingly') + āsanna (Skt. āsanna 'near, at hand', past part. of ā-√sad) + pi (Skt. api 'even'); the ati+ā assimilates to acc-.
wordByWord.notes: Single written token containing the emphatic particle pi (api). The point of the whole verse turns on this word: the danger is not merely present but imminent, right beside the devotee — and still it is disregarded.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. te, nominative plural of tad; correlative answering the relative je in line 2.
wordByWord.notes: Subject of gaṇaṁti; picked up by the relative clause 'who are devoted to your feet'.

wordByWord.etymology: na (Skt. na 'not') + vi (Skt. api, here emphatic 'even'); a common Prakrit emphatic negation.
wordByWord.notes: Strong negation: 'do not in the least reckon with it'.

wordByWord.gloss: they count, reckon, heed, take into account
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit gaṇaï < Skt. √gaṇ 'to count, reckon'; 3rd person plural present gaṇaṁti.
wordByWord.notes: Idiomatic 'na gaṇaï' = 'pays no heed to, treats as nothing'. The fearless devotee does not even 'count' the great elephant as a threat.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ye, nominative plural of the relative yad; Prakrit y- > j-.
wordByWord.notes: Opens the relative clause defining 'te' — 'they who have taken refuge…'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit genitive of the 2nd-person pronoun, < Skt. tava/tubhya(m); stem tumha-.
wordByWord.notes: Addressed to Pārśva; qualifies 'pair of feet'.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit calaṇa < Skt. caraṇa 'foot' (from √car 'to move'); intervocalic -r- > -l-.
wordByWord.notes: First member of the compound calaṇa-juala (the glossary's calaṇa-juala); the object of refuge.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit juala < Skt. yugala 'pair, couple'; y- > j- and -g- elided to a glide.
wordByWord.notes: Accusative object of samallīṇā ('clinging to the pair of feet').

wordByWord.gloss: O Lord of sages! (a vocative epithet of Pārśva)
wordByWord.etymology: muṇi (Skt. muni 'sage') + vaï (Skt. pati 'lord, master'); muni-pati, vocative.
wordByWord.notes: Direct address to Pārśvanātha as chief among ascetics; anchors the whole sentence as a prayer to him.

wordByWord.gloss: lofty, exalted, high (qualifying the pair of feet)
wordByWord.notes: The feet are 'lofty' — spiritually exalted, and pointedly set against the towering height of the great elephant.

wordByWord.gloss: having taken firm refuge in, closely clinging to, wholly resorting to (nominative plural)
wordByWord.etymology: Past participle, Skt. sam-ā-līna (sam + ā + √lī 'to cling, cleave, become absorbed'); the conjunct -lī- doubles to -ll-.
wordByWord.notes: Predicate of 'je…te': those who are 'clung-fast' to the feet. A deliberate counter to accāsannaṁ — the elephant draws 'very near', but the devotee is 'clung-close' to the feet, and the second nearness cancels the first.

literalTranslation: A terrible great elephant — even though it is very close at hand — they do not heed it at all: those who, O Lord of sages, cling fast to your lofty pair of feet.
idiomaticTranslation: Though a dreadful, mighty elephant looms right at their side, they give it not a thought — those souls who cleave for refuge to your two exalted feet, O Lord of sages.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā is the 'turn' that completes the elephant-peril pair: where the preceding verse painted the beast in all its sensory terror — moon-white tusks like pestles, the long swinging trunk, tawny eyes, the roar of a rain-laden cloud — this verse withdraws the camera from the elephant entirely and fixes it on the unmoved devotee. The poet's art lies in a single opposition of two 'nearnesses'. The elephant is accāsanna, 'exceedingly near'; the devotee is samallīṇa, 'clung-close' to Pārśva's feet. Both words are built on coming-near, but the refuge-nearness simply overrides the danger-nearness, so that the verse can end not with rescue from the elephant but with sheer indifference to it: te na vi gaṇaṁti, 'they do not even reckon with it.' The idiom na gaṇaï is precise — the threat is not fought off or fled; it is dismissed as not worth counting. Note too the vertical play: the feet are tuṅga, 'lofty', literally towering, deliberately matched against the towering bulk of the mahā-gaïnda, so that the exaltation of the refuge out-measures the height of the menace. The vocative muṇivaï ('Lord of sages') keeps the whole sentence a direct address to Pārśva, so the fearlessness is never the devotee's own bravado but a property lent by the One clung to. Within the verse-pair structure of the hymn this gāthā thus seals the elephant danger and readies the litany to move on to its next terror.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse crystallises śaraṇa (refuge) and the abhaya (fearlessness) that flows from it. The vehicle of rescue here is specifically the calaṇa-j2uala — the Jina's feet taken as refuge — rather than the name-as-mantra emphasised elsewhere in the hymn; the two are complementary modes of the same protective recollection. Doctrinally the deliverance is inward: fear (bhaya) is treated as a state of mind dissolved by equanimity, not as an external force magically repelled. One 'absorbed' (sam-ā-līna) in the Jina's feet has so steadied the citta that even imminent death does not register as a threat — the practitioner's samatā, not the elephant's behaviour, is what changes. This locates the verse squarely in the Jain understanding of the Tīrthaṅkara as abhaya-dāyaka, the giver of fearlessness, whose refuge confers the inner freedom that worldly peril cannot touch.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: When some danger presses in 'very near' — an accident, an illness, a moment of panic — the reciter is taught not to bargain or flee in the mind but to do exactly what the verse describes: cling fast, inwardly, to Pārśva's feet, and let the threat go un-'counted'. The verse trains the response of steadiness-by-refuge: turn toward the object of devotion rather than fixating on the peril, and fear loses its grip.

crossReferences.text: Namiūṇa (Bhayahara) Stotra v.18 — the summary that gathers disease, water, fire, serpent, thief, foe, lion and elephant and declares that merely reciting Pārśva's name stills them all
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

crossReferences.text: Namiūṇa (Bhayahara) Stotra v.14 — the immediately preceding verse, which paints the rutting elephant whose terror this verse dissolves
crossReferences.source: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha ed.)

sources.title: Parshvanatha (Wikipedia)
sources.consultedFor: Pārśvanātha as the 23rd Tīrthaṅkara addressed here as muṇivaï, and his role as giver of refuge/fearlessness

sources.title: Bhaktamara Stotra (Wikipedia) — on Ācārya Mānatuṅga
sources.consultedFor: traditional attribution of the fear-removing stotras to Ācārya Mānatuṅga, the same poet credited with this hymn

sources.title: Ratnachandra, An Illustrated Ardha-Magadhi Dictionary (Sacitra Arddha-Māgadhī kosha), 1923–1932
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit glosses and Sanskrit derivations for gaïnda (gajendra), calaṇa (caraṇa), juala (yugala), samallīṇa (sam-ā-līna), gaṇaï (√gaṇ)

notes: Word readings follow the supplied book text verbatim (ગઇંદં, ચલણ જુઅલં, સમલ્લીણા, અચ્ચાસન્નંપિ); other recensions print minor variants (e.g. gayanda/gaïnda, samalliṇā/samallīṇā). gaïndaṁ is taken as Skt. gajendra ('lord of elephants') rather than a plain 'elephant', consistent with mahā- intensification. Reliable open online word-by-word editions of this gāthā are scarce and several web 'translations' are unreliable/AI-generated; the gloss here rests on standard Prakrit morphology and the cited dictionary rather than on a single published verse-translation, so individual nuances (especially the exact force of accāsanna and the predicate samallīṇā) carry mild residual uncertainty. The preface notes vv.12–15 as the elephant peril 'extending to the lion'; this particular gāthā names only the elephant, the lion being implied by the section rather than stated in v.15.
